content.problemStatement:
There are NNN people numbered from 111 to NNN such that:


**Exactly one** of these people is a thief and always lies;
All the others are honest and always tell the truth.
If the ithi^{th}ith person claims that the thief is **one** person out of Li,Li+1,Li+2,⋯ ,RiL_i, L_i+1, L_i+2, \cdots, R_iLi​,Li​+1,Li​+2,⋯,Ri​, determine how many people could be the thief.

It is guaranteed that at least one person can be the thief.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
First line of each test case will contain NNN, the number of people. NNN lines follow.
The ithi^{th}ith line contains two space-separated integers - LiL_iLi​ and RiR_iRi​, the range of people amongst which the ithi^{th}ith person claims the thief is.


Output Format

For each test case, in the first line, output KKK, the number of possible thieves.
In the next KKK lines, output the list of people that could be the thieves, in **ascending order**.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases fits in the time limit
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of people. Then N lines follow, the i‑th line contains two integers Li and Ri (1‑based inclusive) – the range claimed by the i‑th person that the thief lies in.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, first print K – the number of people that could be the thief. Then print K lines, each containing one possible thief in ascending order.

sampleTestCases.explanation: If person 1 is the thief, the other statements intersect to [1,2] which contains 1. If person 2 is the thief, the intersection of the remaining statements is also [1,2] containing 2. No other person satisfies the condition.
All truthful statements say the thief is person 1, therefore only person 1 can be the liar.
Same reasoning as the previous sample; only person 1 fits.

solution.approach: If person i is the liar (the thief), then all other N‑1 statements are true. The true statements together imply that the thief must belong to the intersection of all ranges except i's. Let maxL be the maximum left endpoint and minR be the minimum right endpoint among those N‑1 ranges. The intersection is non‑empty iff maxL <= minR. Person i can be the thief only when this intersection contains i itself, i.e. maxL <= i <= minR. We can compute maxL and minR for every i in O(N) using prefix and suffix aggregates: prefMaxL[i] = max(L1..Li), prefMinR[i] = min(R1..Ri) and similarly suffix arrays. For each i we combine the prefix of i‑1 and suffix of i+1 to obtain the required maxL and minR. All candidates are collected and printed. The algorithm runs in linear time per test case and uses O(N) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary arrays for prefix and suffix values.
